attributes.stem: An 81-year-old man with a 50-pack year smoking history and prior alcohol abuse suffers a rapid deterioration 24 hours following a colectomy and end colostomy for a perforated, obstructing rectal cancer. His respiratory rate is 30/min, O2 saturation is 86% and temperature 37.4C. On examination you note increased work of breathing, a mid-line trachea, no wheeze or stridor, and bilateral crackles from lung bases to the midzones. The blood pressure is 100/60mmHg and pulse rate 105/min and regular. Heart sounds are dual with no murmur appreciated. There is peripheral pitting oedema to the mid calf. A chest X-ray is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Fluid overload is the most appropriate response. There is increased opacity in both lower lung fields with increasing density towards the bases, loss of the full cardiac silhouette, and increased prominence of the upper lobe vessels. It is difficult to fully assess the costophrenic recesses or identify septal (Kerley) lines in this radiograph. However, these findings are all consistent with fluid overload. A trial of diuretic therapy and subsequent resolution of hypoxia, respiratory distress and repeat imaging showing resolution would help to confirm this diagnosis. Other clinical signs of fluid overload should also be assessed for (eg. sacral oedema, elevated JVP) to correlate with this imaging.

Basal atelectasis is not the most appropriate response. Due to widespread opacification, it can be difficult to fully assess for changes associated with atelectasis. However, there does not appear to be crowding of the pulmonary vasculature due to reduced lung expansions which is a common sign in atelectasis. There does not appear to be any mediastinal shift, which can occur in atelectasis. Displacement of interlobar fissures and elevation of the hemidiaphragm are also common signs, however, are difficult to assess in this radiograph.

Right lower lobe pneumonia is not the most appropriate response. Consolidation and changes associated with a right lower lobe pneumonia may be present, however, such changes often have a more 'patchy' appearance than seen. It would also not account for the bilateral changes that are seen in the radiograph. As such, this is not the most likely diagnosis.

Pulmonary embolus is not the most appropriate response. It is important to note that a chest radiograph is a poor tool for identifying pulomary emboli - the main role it serves in suspected PE is to rule out other causes of chest pain/hypoxia/respiratory distress. A PE could certainly account for the clinical presentation of the patient given in the stem, however, given the presence of abnormal findings on the chest x-ray this would not be our primary suspicion. The PERC rule and Well's Criteria should be applied to this patient to assess for the risk of PE, as well as ensuring he has been adequately anticoagulated throughout his hospital stay, and assesses for signs suggestive of a DVT.

Adult respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is not the most appropriate response. ARDS can be difficult to differntiate from fluid overload, as both can appear quite similar with bilateral opacification of the lung fields, though in ARDS this may be asymmetric. In ARDS the opacification more commonly has a 'ground-glass' appearance, which is not seen in this radiograph. Additionally, ARDS results due to damage to alveoli endothelium, which is typically secondary to sepsis, severe pneumonia, traumatic lung injury and aspiration of gastric contents - none of which were known issues in our patient. Another criteria for diagnosis is that the presentation must not be explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload, which this presentation could be. As such, this is not the most likely diagnosis.
learningPoints: Radiographs should have a structured approach and findings correlated with the clinical picture
references: Khan, A. N., Al-Jahdali, H., Al-Ghanem, S., & Gouda, A. (2009). Reading chest radiographs in the critically ill (Part I): Normal chest radiographic appearance, instrumentation and complications from instrumentation. Annals of thoracic medicine, 4(2), 75–87. https://doi.org/10.4103/1817-1737.49416

Khan, A. N., Al-Jahdali, H., Al-Ghanem, S., & Gouda, A. (2009). Reading chest radiographs in the critically ill (Part II): Radiography of lung pathologies common in the ICU patient. Annals of thoracic medicine, 4(3), 149–157. https://doi.org/10.4103/1817-1737.53349
